value.prior_session_context: Tuesday 2026-06-23 saw a global technology and semiconductor selloff; the KOSPI plunged roughly 10%, its sharpest one-day drop since March, and the Nikkei also fell. June 24 was the first stabilization session after that rout.

text: Wednesday, June 24, 2026 was a regular trading day for the referenced Asia-Pacific cash-equity markets. The session followed a bruising Tuesday June 23, when a global technology and semiconductor selloff sent the KOSPI down roughly 10% in its sharpest one-day drop since March and pushed the Nikkei lower as well. All referenced markets operated on their normal Wednesday schedules; there were no market holidays.

nikkei_225.basis: WSJ market data and MarketWatch/FactSet close; previous close 69,788.38

kospi.basis: Yonhap close bulletin; MarketWatch corroborates rounded +3.3%

hang_seng.basis: MarketWatch/FactSet close; Yahoo Finance At close 4:08 PM GMT+8

value.basis_note: MSCI Asia ex-Japan gained 0.04% per Reuters

calculation.method: Nikkei: WSJ market data shows 69,174.97 minus previous close 69,788.38 equals -613.41 points (-0.88%); KOSPI: Yonhap gives +267.18 points to 8,471.02 (+3.26%); Hang Seng: MarketWatch/Yahoo show +75.90 to 23,412.18 (+0.33%); Shanghai Composite and ASX 200 per MarketWatch/FactSet rounded percents

text: Asia-Pacific equity markets closed mixed on Wednesday, June 24, 2026, after a global technology selloff the prior session. The Nikkei 225 fell 613.41 points (-0.88%) to 69,174.97, the KOSPI surged 267.18 points (+3.26%) to 8,471.02, the Hang Seng rose 75.90 points (+0.33%) to 23,412.18, and the Shanghai Composite added 0.11% to 4,110.81. MarketWatch also put the S&P/ASX 200 up 0.24% to 8,808.40 and Singapore's FTSE Straits Times up 0.20% to 5,216.44. MSCI's index of Asian equities outside Japan rose 0.04%.

value.prior_session_context: Tuesday June 23 KOSPI plunge was the sharpest one-day drop since March, driven by global semiconductor and AI-valuation selloff
value.intraday_volatility: KOSPI opened higher, briefly fell into negative territory on heavy foreign selling, and dropped close to 8,000 before reversing course on retail and institutional buying

calculation.method: Yonhap close bulletin gives KOSPI at 8,471.02 up 267.18 points (+3.26%); Reuters independently confirms the +3.26% figure and notes the prior session 10% plunge; trade volume was 413.4 million shares worth 53.78 trillion won

text: South Korea's KOSPI rebounded 267.18 points, or 3.26%, to close at 8,471.02 on Wednesday, June 24, after choppy trading. The rebound followed a roughly 10% crash on Tuesday, June 23, the market's sharpest one-day drop since March. Foreign investors sold a net 4.66 trillion won, but retail and institutional buyers purchased 2.63 trillion and 1.92 trillion won respectively. Samsung Electronics jumped 9.84% and SK hynix rose 0.98%. The Korean won weakened to 1,541.8 per dollar.

value.yen_context: Yen hovered near 40-year lows, keeping markets on edge over potential currency intervention

calculation.method: WSJ market data shows Nikkei 225 at 69,174.97, down 613.41 points (-0.88%) from previous close of 69,788.38; MarketWatch independently confirms -0.88% to 69,174.97

text: Japan's Nikkei 225 fell for a second consecutive day on Wednesday, June 24, closing down 613.41 points or 0.88% at 69,174.97. The index traded in a wide 68,461.10 to 70,218.71 intraday range, reflecting volatile conditions. The yen traded around 161.81 per dollar, near 40-year lows, keeping markets on edge over potential intervention. MarketWatch noted the decline was part of a mixed regional pattern where Japanese shares lagged while Hong Kong and Seoul rose.

hang_seng.basis: MarketWatch/FactSet and Yahoo Finance close at 4:08 PM GMT+8

value.hang_seng_leaders: MarketWatch listed Semicon Mfg Intl +8.93%, WuXi AppTec +8.33%, and Wuxi Biologics +4.17% among top Hang Seng gainers

market_session: Hong Kong and Shanghai regular cash-equity closes, Wednesday 2026-06-24

calculation.method: MarketWatch/FactSet close: Hang Seng at 23,412.18 (+0.33%) and Shanghai Composite at 4,110.81 (+0.11%); AP independently corroborated both rounded levels

text: Greater China markets stabilized on Wednesday, June 24. The Hang Seng rose 75.90 points (+0.33%) to 23,412.18, led by semiconductor and biotech constituents including Semicon Manufacturing Intl up 8.93%, WuXi AppTec up 8.33%, and Wuxi Biologics up 4.17%. The Shanghai Composite added 0.11% to 4,110.81. Both moves were modest stabilization bounces after the prior session's technology-led decline.

subject.name: Post-tech-selloff stabilization with AI valuation stress, oil declining, and dollar at one-year high

value.primary_driver: Stabilization after Tuesday global tech/semiconductor selloff; bargain hunting in battered markets (KOSPI +3.26%) versus continued pressure on Japanese equities
value.tech_selloff_context: Global semiconductor and AI-related shares fell Tuesday on concerns about stretched valuations and elevated US rate expectations; WSJ/S&P 500 fell 1.4% and Nasdaq fell 2.2% on Tuesday June 23
value.macro_support: Oil prices extended losses toward four-month lows as US-Iran negotiations progressed; Brent settled at $73.74 down 4.33%, AP reported Brent at $73.87 down 3.8% and US crude at $70.34 down 3.9%
value.dollar_strength: Dollar index rose 0.24% to 101.63, highest since May 2025; yen near 40-year lows at ~161.81
value.boj_context: BOJ summary released Wednesday showed some board members called for further rate hikes after the central bank raised rates to a 31-year high of 1.00%
value.risk_events_ahead: Micron Technology earnings after US close Wednesday; US PCE inflation data due Thursday (expected 4.1% YoY for May, highest in three years)

market_session: Asia-Pacific regular sessions and global macro context, Wednesday 2026-06-24
text: The Asia session on June 24 was a post-selloff stabilization after Tuesday's global technology rout. The KOSPI rebounded 3.26% on bargain hunting after plunging roughly 10% the prior day, while the Nikkei fell for a second day (-0.88%). Macro support came from further declines in oil prices (Brent settled down 4.33% at $73.74) as US-Iran negotiations progressed, but the dollar hit a one-year high (index at 101.63) and the yen traded near 40-year lows around 161.81. A BOJ summary showed some members pushing for further rate hikes after the 1.00% hike. Analysts cautioned about renewed volatility, with Micron earnings and US PCE data as the next risk events.

value.cross_market_context: normal regular session; European equities steadied near the flatline after the prior session's tech-led selloff, with the STOXX 600 closing marginally higher while the major bourses finished mixed; idiosyncratic defence (Rheinmetall) and real estate (Segro) moves dominated single-stock action

text: European equity markets traded a normal regular session on Wednesday, June 24, 2026, and closed mixed after the prior session's tech-led selloff, with the pan-European STOXX 600 ending marginally higher. Live closing prints are evidenced by the LSEG FTSE 100 daily table (close as of 16:35 BST on volume of about 960 million shares) and dated June 24 closes across the Trading Economics Euro Area, Germany and France pages, plus the Reuters European close wrap. Daily moves are measured against the Tuesday, June 23 close. There is no cross-market calendar asymmetry: both European and US venues traded a normal session.

stoxx_europe_600.basis: Trading Economics dated EU600 table 635.16 / +0.08%; Reuters wrap closed 0.1% higher; MarketScreener widget 635.16, +0.08% (prior 634.63); corroborated across vendors

euro_stoxx_50.basis: Trading Economics EU50 table 6,219.46 / -0.18% (previous 6,230.55); Reuters/MarketScreener stocks-mentioned table 6,214.70 / -0.25%; CFD-tracked; both readings down about 0.2%

dax.basis: Trading Economics dated daily summary DE40 24,712.70 / -0.73% (previous 24,893.58); Reuters European cash close -0.6%; Trading Economics live CFD about 24,740 / -0.62% into the US session; disclosed basis discrepancy, magnitude bracketed -0.6% to -0.73%

cac_40.basis: Trading Economics FR40 8,385.49 / +0.54% (close about 8,385); snapped a three-session losing streak; corroborated by the Euro Area cross-index table

market_session: European regular close on 2026-06-24; FTSE 100 at the 16:35 BST London close

calculation.method: FTSE 100: June 24 close 10,461.63 minus June 23 close 10,428.85 equals +32.78 points (+0.31%); CAC 40: 8,385.49 minus 8,340.71 equals +44.78 (+0.54%); STOXX 600: 635.16 minus 634.64 equals +0.52 (about +0.08%); continental percentages are Trading Economics CFD-tracked day-changes corroborated by the Reuters wrap, except the DAX where Reuters cites -0.6% at the cash close versus the Trading Economics daily-summary -0.73%

text: European equities closed mixed on Wednesday, June 24, 2026, steadying after the prior session's tech-led selloff. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 edged up 0.08% to 635.16 (Reuters: closed 0.1% higher). France's CAC 40 was the standout, rising 0.54% to 8,385.49 and snapping a three-session losing streak on a luxury rebound, and the UK's FTSE 100 added 0.31% to a precise 10,461.63. The EURO STOXX 50 eased 0.18% to 6,219.46 (Reuters/MarketScreener -0.25% to 6,214.70). Germany's DAX was the laggard and carries a disclosed basis discrepancy: the Trading Economics dated daily summary put the DE40 at 24,712.70, down 0.73% from 24,893.58, while the Reuters European cash close had the DAX down 0.6% and the Trading Economics live CFD sat near 24,740 (-0.62%) into the US session. Daily moves are measured against the June 23 close.

market_session: London regular session close, Wednesday 2026-06-24 (16:35 BST)

calculation.method: close 10,461.63 minus prior close 10,428.85 equals +32.78 points; +32.78 / 10,428.85 equals +0.31 percent

text: The FTSE 100 is the cycle's one precise official close and a relative outperformer: on the Investors Chronicle/LSEG historical daily table it opened 10,429.02, traded a 10,406.95 to 10,469.33 range, and closed 10,461.63 on volume of 960,305,976 shares, up 32.78 points (+0.31%) from Tuesday's 10,428.85. London outperformed as the continental benchmarks closed mixed, with Germany's DAX lower and France's CAC 40 higher.

subject.name: Muted, stabilizing session as Iran de-escalation lifts cyclicals while defence and commodities weigh

value.primary_support: investors assessed US-Iran de-escalation, easing energy prices and lifting consumer-discretionary and real estate names, offsetting weakness in defence, commodities and banks
triggers: US-Iran peace negotiations and improving Strait of Hormuz shipping
Germany scrapping a landmark F126 frigate programme, sinking Rheinmetall
awaited Micron earnings for chip-sector cues
value.rates: traders priced another 25 bps ECB rate hike by year-end per LSEG data
value.oil: Brent crude hit its lowest since the US-Israeli war on Iran began as supply-disruption concerns eased
value.macro: German Ifo business morale rose in June to its best current-situation reading in nearly two years

text: European shares were muted on Wednesday, steadying after the prior tech-led selloff as investors assessed US-Iran negotiations. Per Reuters, de-escalation and improving Strait of Hormuz shipping pushed Brent crude to its lowest since the US-Israeli war on Iran began, which weighed on energy and miners but eased rate pressure and lifted cyclicals. The dominant idiosyncratic event was Germany scrapping plans to build six F126 frigates, sending defence champion Rheinmetall to its biggest one-day drop on record and dragging aerospace and defence shares. German Ifo business morale rose in June to its best current-situation reading in nearly two years, and markets priced another 25 bps ECB hike by year-end. Investors awaited Micron's results for fresh chip-sector direction.

leaders.real_estate_note: Segro +17.4% after Prologis took its 16.6 billion dollar bid public; biggest intraday jump since March 2009
leaders.consumer_discretionary_note: luxury and retail rebounded - Adidas, LVMH, Hermes, Inditex up to 3%

laggards.commodity_note: tracking lower metals and oil prices
laggards.tech_note: reversed gains after the prior session's near-five-month-worst drop

text: The tape was a rotation out of defence, commodities and banks into real estate and consumer discretionary. Per Reuters, real estate rose 3% led by Segro's 17.4% surge after Prologis took its 16.6 billion dollar bid public (its biggest intraday jump since March 2009), while miners fell 2.5% and energy 2.3% on lower metals and oil, aerospace and defence lost 0.8%, industrials eased 0.2%, and tech reversed gains to fall 0.3% ahead of Micron. On Trading Economics data, consumer-discretionary names rebounded (Adidas, LVMH, Hermes, Inditex up to 3%) while banks fell as markets took profits. Sector percentages are carried at medium confidence; index-level closes are high confidence.

decliners.Rheinmetall_basis: Reuters -18.7% biggest one-day drop on record; Trading Economics component -14.79% to 996.60; intraday reports -13% to -19%
decliners.chips_note: Reuters cash close Infineon -1.2%, BE Semiconductor -1.3%, ASML -0.5%; Trading Economics later CFD had Infineon +1.29% and ASML -0.45% - cash-close vs CFD basis disclosed

text: The day's epicentre was Rheinmetall, which fell sharply - Reuters put it at -18.7%, its biggest one-day drop on record (Trading Economics component -14.79% to 996.60) - after Germany scrapped its F126 frigate programme; counterpart TKMS jumped 16%. The other standout was Segro, up 17.4% after Prologis took its 16.6 billion dollar bid public. Luxury and aerospace led the gainers: L'Oreal +2.83% (on a Jefferies target-price raise), Safran +2.20%, Air Liquide +1.99%, LVMH +1.92%, Hermes +1.50%, Inditex +1.12%, Kering about +1% and Airbus +0.95%. Decliners included TotalEnergies -1.84% on lower oil, Thales -1.2% on the defence drag, and Argenx about -7% on a drug-trial setback. Chipmakers were mixed by basis: Reuters cash close had Infineon -1.2%, BE Semiconductor -1.3% and ASML -0.5%, while the later Trading Economics CFD snapshot showed Infineon +1.29% and ASML -0.45%. Single-stock percentages are carried at medium confidence.

value.comparison_basis: US daily moves measured vs Tuesday 2026-06-23
value.dominant_driver: crude oil's slide to a four-month low (US-Iran de-escalation and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz) eased inflation fears and pulled US Treasury yields lower across the curve
value.secondary_backdrop: still-hawkish Warsh Fed with the market pricing a second 2026 rate hike, and a US dollar at a 13-month high
value.context: first global macro session after the June 23 tech-led equity selloff

text: June 24, 2026 was a normal Wednesday global macro session; US rate, FX and commodity moves are measured against the prior regular session of Tuesday, June 23, and the official Treasury par-yield CSV read live has its newest row dated 06/24/2026 directly after the 06/23/2026 row. The day's macro tape was set by one dominant cross-asset driver - crude oil's slide to a four-month low, as US-Iran de-escalation and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz eased supply and inflation fears - which pulled US Treasury yields lower across the curve, against a still-hawkish Federal Reserve backdrop (the market now pricing a second 2026 hike) and a US dollar at a 13-month high.

value.shape: bull flattening led by the belly and long end
value.basis_note: the +30 bp figure is the official constant-maturity par-yield basis; Reuters' market-based 2s/10s closed near 25 bp, its flattest since March 2025

calculation.method: read official Treasury par-yield CSV live; 10Y minus 2Y; change vs the 06/23/2026 row

text: US Treasury par yields tumbled across the curve on June 24, closing at 4.11% (2Y), 4.15% (3Y), 4.17% (5Y), 4.28% (7Y), 4.41% (10Y), 4.87% (20Y) and 4.86% (30Y) - down roughly 5 to 10 bp from the June 23 close (2Y 4.16%, 10Y 4.50%, 30Y 4.94%), with the belly and long end leading. That is a bull flattening: the 2s10s slope on the official constant-maturity basis narrowed to +30 bp from +34 bp, and Reuters put the market-based 2s/10s near 25 bp, its flattest since March 2025. The long end kept its small kink, with the 20Y (4.87%) 1 bp above the 30Y (4.86%). Falling yields were the day's macro relief signal, driven by oil's slide easing inflation fears.

value.us_10y_breakeven_note: below the 5y, its lowest since April 2025
value.driver: oil and energy prices tumbling as the Middle East conflict cools and supply routes reopen
value.significance: explains why nominal yields fell even though realized PCE inflation is set to print a three-year high on June 25

text: The mechanism behind the yield drop was collapsing market-based inflation expectations. Reuters reported the US 5-year breakeven inflation rate at 2.20%, its lowest this year, with the 10-year breakeven below that at its lowest since April 2025; one-year euro-area inflation swaps fell back below the ECB's 2% target and the 2-year UK inflation swap hit a six-month low. Falling energy prices as the Middle East conflict cools are pulling forward-looking inflation pricing down rapidly - which is why nominal Treasury yields fell even though realized PCE inflation is expected to print a three-year high on Thursday.

value.brent_basis: Trading Economics CFD June 24; corroborated by AP ~$73.87 and Reuters' four-month-low framing
value.low_marker: four-month low; lowest since late February / since the start of the Iran war
drivers: more tankers moving out of the Strait of Hormuz
US-Iran de-escalation; Trump said Iran sought no transit tolls
UAE exporting at ~85% of pre-war levels (IEA)
value.us_inventory_note: EIA US crude inventories at their lowest since 1984, Cushing below operational minimums

calculation.method: vendor CFD/settlement level with cross-source corroboration; day change vs June 23

text: Crude oil was the engine of the day's macro move, sliding to a four-month low. Brent traded near $73.97/bbl on June 24, down about 4% on the day (Trading Economics CFD; AP put the Brent close near $73.87 and Reuters flagged the lowest level since the Iran war began), with WTI near $70. The fall - roughly 23% over the month and about 40% below the wartime peak - was driven by more tankers moving out of the Strait of Hormuz after US-Iran de-escalation, with President Trump saying Iran sought no transit tolls and the IEA estimating the UAE is exporting at about 85% of pre-war levels. The supply-normalization narrative held even with US EIA crude inventories at their lowest since 1984. Oil's drop is the disinflationary impulse that pulled yields and inflation expectations down.

value.characterization: rose for a sixth straight session to a 13-month high
value.ice_dxy_level_basis: Trading Economics ICE DXY 101.13 at the June 23 close (+0.11%) and ~101.1 in early June 24 trade
value.level_confidence: precise June 24 close carried at medium confidence given vendor/snapshot basis differences
value.driver: hawkish Fed repricing (a second 2026 hike now priced) plus safe-haven demand during the tech rout
value.global_note: Reuters: the dollar's surge is causing less global stress than usual because tumbling oil offsets its inflationary bite abroad

calculation.method: Reuters daily characterization (direction, streak, 13-month high) with Trading Economics ICE DXY level basis; precise close cross-vendor

text: The dollar ground higher again. Reuters reported the dollar index rose for a sixth straight session to a 13-month high, driven by hawkish Fed repricing and safe-haven demand during the week's tech rout; Trading Economics' ICE DXY basis showed the index at 101.13 at the June 23 close and near 101.1 in early June 24 trade, with the June 24 close reported around 101.6 - the precise level carried at medium confidence given vendor and snapshot differences. The strong dollar is the tightening-leaning half of the macro picture, though Reuters noted its surge is causing less global stress than usual because tumbling oil offsets its inflationary bite abroad.

value.fed_funds_pricing: traders adding to bets of a second 25 bp Fed hike by end-December (CME FedWatch); previously a single 25 bp rise was priced
value.fed_stance: hawkish; curve flattening accelerated after last week's Fed statement and Chair Warsh's press conference
value.pce_headline_marker: would be the highest since April 2023
value.forward_view: economists widely expect May to mark this year's inflation peak as lower oil and fading tariff effects curb pressures

calculation.method: FactSet/UBS/BofA consensus for the May PCE release; BEA official prior prints; CME FedWatch pricing

text: The monetary backdrop stayed restrictive. After last week's hawkish Fed statement and Chair Kevin Warsh's press conference, traders added to bets of a second 25 bp rate hike by end-December on CME's FedWatch tool, up from a single rise previously. The next catalyst is Thursday's May PCE price index (released June 25, 8:30 a.m. EDT), the Fed's preferred gauge, which economists expect to rise 4.1% year over year - the highest since April 2023 and up from 3.8% in April (BEA's official series rose from 2.9% in January to 3.8% in April) - with core PCE seen at 3.3%. Economists widely expect May to mark this year's inflation peak, since the very oil decline driving markets today should curb price pressures going forward.

value.rate_marker: highest since September 1995; first hike since December
value.dissent: board member Asada Toichiro dissented, citing downside risks to output and employment
value.summary_released: 2026-06-24 (Summary of Opinions from the June 15-16 meeting)
value.summary_signal: one member said it is appropriate to continue raising the policy rate as underlying CPI approaches 2% and conditions stay accommodative; some members called for faster hikes
value.original_rationale: capping the Iran-war-driven energy shock - now being undercut as oil collapses
value.yen_note: yen languished near multi-decade lows against the surging dollar despite the hike

text: Globally, central banks stayed in tightening mode. The Bank of Japan's Summary of Opinions from its June 15-16 meeting, released Wednesday, affirmed its rate-hike stance: at that meeting the BOJ lifted its policy rate 25 bp to 1.0% in a 7-1 vote (board member Asada Toichiro dissenting) - the highest since September 1995 and its first hike since December - and one member said it is appropriate to keep raising the rate as underlying inflation approaches 2%, with some members calling for a faster pace. The original rationale, capping an Iran-war energy shock, is now being undercut by the oil collapse, yet the yen still languished near multi-decade lows against the 13-month-high dollar, a classic rate-differential and dollar-strength story.

value.gold_spot_marker: slipped below $4,000/oz on a spot basis (Reuters), its lowest this year
value.gold_basis: COMEX future held just above $4,000 (MarketWatch ~$4,018.80, +0.25%) while Reuters spot dipped below - spot-vs-future basis disclosed

vix.note: VIX fell, consistent with risk stabilization

calculation.method: Reuters spot characterization with MarketWatch COMEX future cross-check; month change vs end-May

text: Precious metals were the casualty of the strong dollar and hawkish Fed. Gold hovered around the $4,000/oz mark - Reuters said spot slipped below $4,000 to its lowest this year, while the COMEX future held just above at about $4,018.80 (+0.25%, MarketWatch), a disclosed spot-versus-future basis gap - and is down roughly 12% in June, its worst month since 2008. Silver fell about 8% on the day and is down some 55% from its January peak. The 13-month-high dollar, repricing toward a second Fed hike, and the fading Middle East safe-haven bid drove the metals lower; the VIX, by contrast, fell to 18.63 (-4.4%), consistent with the day's risk stabilization. These daily metals moves are carried at medium confidence.

value.core_tension: a fresh oil-led disinflation impulse (yields and inflation expectations down) against still-hawkish central banks (Fed pricing a second hike, BOJ affirming more) and a 13-month-high dollar
value.key_dependency: the disinflation trade hinges on Middle East de-escalation holding - continued Strait of Hormuz tanker flows and no transit tolls; a flare-up would snap oil and the risk premium back
value.next_catalyst: Thursday's May PCE print decides the rates leg
value.caveat: de-escalation and tanker-flow normalization are evolving and carried at medium confidence

text: Net read: June 24's macro state was a one-way disinflation impulse colliding with a hawkish policy backdrop. Crude's slide to a four-month low pulled Treasury yields down across the curve, flattened the 2s/10s to its lowest since March 2025, dragged market inflation expectations to the year's lows, and sent gold below $4,000 - while the same hawkish-Fed repricing that has the market pricing a second 2026 hike kept the dollar grinding to a 13-month high and the BOJ affirming further tightening. The whole disinflation trade rests on Middle East de-escalation holding: continued Strait of Hormuz tanker flows and Trump's statement that Iran seeks no tolls. A flare-up would snap oil and the risk premium back; absent that, Thursday's May PCE print - expected at a three-year-high 4.1% - decides whether the front end can keep pace with the falling long end. De-escalation specifics are evolving and carried at medium confidence.

market_session: regular US cash-equity session, third session after Juneteenth closure
text: The June 24, 2026 report date was a regular Wednesday U.S. cash-equity session, the third session after the Juneteenth and weekend break, so sentiment values can be read against a live U.S. closing tape.

value.context: Mixed close: more stocks rose than fell within the S&P 500 despite index decline; tech giants including Microsoft weighed; Brent crude dropped 3.8%; Treasury yields fell; rotation into cyclicals away from mega-cap tech

text: AP reported a mixed June 24 U.S. close: the S&P 500 fell 7.24 points or 0.1% to 7,358.22 and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 110.40 points or 0.4% to 25,476.64 as losses for tech giants including Microsoft weighed on the market. The Dow rose 182.06 points or 0.4% to 51,848.90 and the Russell 2000 gained 11.15 points or 0.4% to 2,986.63. AP noted that more stocks rose than fell within the S&P 500 despite the index decline, signaling internal breadth improvement even as the headline stayed negative.

subject.name: CNN Fear & Greed Index
subject.source_basis: CNN Fear & Greed page corroborated by Finhacker.cz CNN-tracker

value.methodology_note: CNN Stock Price Breadth indicator still reflects the McClellan Summation Index methodology change noted in prior reports
value.freshness_note: Neither publisher had posted a verified June 24 post-close update at collection time; CNN 27 reflects an early-June-24 pre-market snapshot while Finhacker 28 is the June 23 post-close value

market_session: latest available readings at collection time
text: The Fear & Greed Index remained in fear territory on June 24. CNN's page displayed 27 (fear) with 'Previous close 27', '1 week ago 32', '1 month ago 58', and 'Last updated Jun 24 at 5:18:16 AM ET'. Finhacker.cz's CNN-tracker still showed 28 (fear) with a data-updated timestamp of 'Jun 23, 2026, 8:00 PM ET'. Neither source carried a verified June 24 post-close update at collection time, so the latest available reading is the CNN 27 value, which is one point below the Finhacker June 23 post-close value of 28.

market_session: Cboe published volatility-index data for the regular US session

calculation.method: VIX one-day percent change = (18.63 - 19.49) / 19.49 * 100 = -4.41%; curve compares Cboe close fields for VIX9D, VIX, VIX3M, and VIX6M on 2026-06-24

text: Yahoo Finance historical data showed VIX closing at 18.63 on June 24, down 0.86 points or 4.41% from 19.49 on June 23, with OHLC of open 19.13, high 20.34, low 18.04, close 18.63. The same-date Cboe volatility curve was upward sloping with a restored normal front: VIX9D at 18.07, VIX at 18.63, VIX3M at 20.37, and VIX6M at 22.40. The VIX9D-to-VIX gap widened to 0.56 points (VIX9D below VIX), restoring the typical upward slope after June 23's near-flat 0.02-point spread. The VIX9D intraday range of 17.00 to 20.67 remained wide, reflecting two-way volatility during the session before the vol-easing close.

market_session: latest AAII weekly survey available for the June 24 report date

calculation.method: Bull-bear spread = bullish 36.6% minus bearish 39.4% = -2.8 percentage points

text: The latest AAII sentiment survey (week ending 6/17/2026, published June 18) showed bullish sentiment at 36.6%, neutral at 24.1%, and bearish at 39.4%, leaving a bull-bear spread of -2.8 percentage points. This is the same reading available for the June 23 report and does not reflect the June 23-24 price action. The next AAII release is expected Thursday June 25.

value.data_freshness_note: No new weekly fund-flow releases verified on the live ICI or ETF.com pages between the June 23 and June 24 report dates; both readings predate the June 23-24 risk-off action

market_session: latest fund-flow releases available for the June 24 report date
text: No new verified weekly fund-flow data was available for the June 24 report date. ICI's latest combined-flow release (published June 17 for the week ended June 10) showed $31.52 billion of total long-term inflows, including $48.33 billion of ETF net issuance, $16.64 billion into equity funds, $18.21 billion into bond funds, and $2.27 billion out of commodity funds. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article reported $119.83 billion of total ETF inflows led by $83.62 billion into U.S. equity ETFs. These readings provide positioning context heading into the June 23-24 action, not a real-time flow gauge.

value.treasury_note: Treasury yields fell in the bond market
value.macro_theme: Iran de-escalation, dollar strength, rate-hike pricing, falling oil

text: Reuters reported Brent crude settled at $73.74, down 4.33% on the day and nearing pre-Iran-war levels as Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic improved. The dollar index rose 0.24% to 101.63, its highest since May 2025, as markets priced in at least one Fed rate hike. Gold fell 3.2% to $3,976.73 on the stronger dollar. Treasury yields fell in the bond market. The macro backdrop drove a rotation into cyclicals, industrials, materials, and consumer discretionary, and out of tech and energy.

value.cash_equity_tape: mixed close with S&P -0.1%, Nasdaq -0.4%, Dow +0.4%, Russell +0.4%; more stocks rose than fell within S&P despite index decline
value.survey_sentiment: net bearish, unchanged from prior survey, does not reflect June 23-24 action
value.volatility: VIX -4.41% to 18.63; upward-sloping curve restored with VIX9D back below VIX
value.flows: latest available flows are pre-selloff; still showing strong equity allocation
value.risk_read: risk-off intensity faded from June 23, but sentiment remains in fear zone and tech/AI overhang persists into Micron earnings

market_session: regular US cash-equity session synthesis from latest available sentiment indicators
text: The best synthesis for June 24 is 'fragile stabilization, tech still bleeding.' June 23's unambiguous risk-off broke on June 24 into a mixed close: the Dow and Russell 2000 each rose 0.4%, AP reported more advancers than decliners within the S&P 500, and the VIX eased 4.41% to 18.63 with the VIX9D-to-VIX spread restoring a normal upward slope. But the S&P 500 still fell 0.1% and the Nasdaq dropped 0.4% as tech giants including Microsoft continued to weigh ahead of Micron earnings. The Fear & Greed Index stayed in fear at 27-28, the AAII survey is unchanged and doesn't reflect the two-day action, and the latest flow readings still predate the selloff. The regime is therefore less risk-off than June 23 but not risk-on — it is a fragile, rotation-driven stabilization with an unresolved tech/AI overhang.

value.prior_session_context: Tuesday 2026-06-23 saw a technology and semiconductor selloff; the S&P 500 fell 1.4% and the Nasdaq Composite fell 2.2%. June 24 was the first session after that rout, and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq closed lower for a third consecutive day.

text: Wednesday, June 24, 2026 was a regular trading day for US cash-equity and futures markets. The session followed Tuesday's global technology and semiconductor selloff, and resulted in a third consecutive decline for the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite. All referenced venues operated on normal Wednesday schedules; there were no market holidays.

sp_500.basis: AP close summary and CNBC close section; previous close 7,365.46

dow.basis: AP close summary; CNBC rounds to +0.35%; AP rounds to +0.4%

nasdaq_composite.basis: AP close summary and CNBC close section; CNBC -0.43% unrounded; previous close 25,587.04

text: US equity indexes closed mixed on Wednesday, June 24, 2026. The S&P 500 fell 7.24 points (-0.1%) to 7,358.22, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 182.06 points (+0.4%) to 51,848.90, the Nasdaq Composite fell 110.40 points (-0.4%) to 25,476.64, and the Russell 2000 rose 11.15 points (+0.4%) to 2,986.63. Week-to-date, the S&P 500 is down 1.9%, the Dow is up 0.6%, the Nasdaq is down 3.9%, and the Russell 2000 is up 0.2%. Year-to-date, the S&P 500 is up 7.5%, the Dow is up 7.9%, the Nasdaq is up 9.6%, and the Russell 2000 is up 20.3%.

value.sp_500_breadth: nearly 2 out of every 3 stocks gained ground despite the index falling 0.1%
value.interpretation: Positive internal breadth diverged from the cap-weighted index decline, indicating that the sell-off was concentrated in large-cap tech rather than broad-based
value.context: AP noted the same pattern on Tuesday: tech stocks pulled the market lower despite broader gains elsewhere

text: The S&P 500 fell 0.1% despite nearly 2 out of every 3 stocks in the index gaining ground, a positive breadth divergence indicating that the decline was driven by large-cap technology weights rather than broad selling. AP explicitly noted this pattern and highlighted that the same dynamic occurred on Tuesday, when tech stocks pulled the market lower despite broader gains.

microsoft.note: Heaviest weight on the market per AP

alphabet.note: Company replacing Verizon in the Dow on Monday; S&P Global announced the change Tuesday

value.context: AP framed these as losses for several influential tech heavyweights including Microsoft that weighed down the broader market even though most stocks gained

text: Large-cap technology stocks were the primary drag on the S&P 500. Microsoft fell 2.3% and was the heaviest weight on the market, Oracle slumped 4.6%, and Alphabet slipped 0.2%. AP noted that declines for several influential tech heavyweights pulled the broader market lower despite positive internal breadth. Alphabet is set to replace Verizon in the Dow Jones Industrial Average on Monday, becoming the fifth Magnificent 7 company in the price-weighted index.

brent_crude.discrepancy_note: AP and CNBC/Reuters cite different settlement levels for Brent on June 24: AP reports $73.87 (-3.8%), while CNBC reports $73.74 (-4.33%). The discrepancy reflects vendor rounding and timing differences in settlement reporting.

wti_crude.note: AP and CNBC agree on the $70.34 settle

value.context: Brent at its lowest level since before the US and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran at the end of February; WTI at its lowest since early March

energy_equities.cnbc_group_note: Exxon Mobil, Chevron, ConocoPhillips and SLB all declined more than 2%; XLE ETF down more than 1%

market_session: NYMEX/ICE regular settlement and US equity close, Wednesday 2026-06-24
text: Oil prices continued their decline as the US and Iran negotiated a possible end to their war. Brent crude settled at its lowest level since before the conflict began: AP reported $73.87 per barrel (-3.8%) while CNBC reported $73.74 (-4.33%), a vendor discrepancy disclosed above. US crude fell to $70.34 (-3.9% per AP, -3.92% per CNBC). Energy equities fell broadly: Exxon Mobil lost 2% and Chevron lost 2.6%, with CNBC reporting ConocoPhillips and SLB also down more than 2% and the XLE energy ETF down more than 1%.

ten_year.note: AP close: 10-year fell to 4.40% from 4.50% late Tuesday; CNBC noted it dropped below 4.5%

value.context: Yields remain elevated from earlier in the year; the 2-year more closely tracks Fed rate expectations; CME FedWatch shows at least one rate hike priced by December

text: Treasury yields mostly fell. The 10-year Treasury yield dropped to 4.40% from 4.50% late Tuesday, and the 2-year yield eased to 4.15% from 4.16%. Despite the decline, yields remain elevated from earlier in the year, especially the 2-year which tracks Fed rate expectations. CME Group data shows Wall Street forecasting at least one rate hike by December.

micron.note: CNBC: shares came off their lows but ended down 0.3% ahead of earnings after the bell; stock ended Tuesday at $1,051.77

sandisk.note: Both Micron and Sandisk tumbled 13% in the previous session

value.smh_etf: VanEck Semiconductor ETF ended marginally lower

micron_earnings_consensus.note: Analysts polled by FactSet see earnings of $20.83 per share on revenue of $35.75 billion

text: Chip and memory stocks stabilized somewhat after Tuesday's rout but still closed lower. Micron Technology ended down 0.3%, coming off its session lows ahead of its fiscal Q3 earnings release after the bell. Analysts polled by FactSet see earnings of $20.83 per share on revenue of $35.75 billion. Fellow memory stock Sandisk dropped 2.5%. Both had tumbled 13% on Tuesday. The VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) ended marginally lower.

homebuilders.note: AP: surged 16.7% after revenue beat; CNBC: up more than 17% intraday, best day since Jan 2022
homebuilders.driver: Approval of legislation beneficial to the homebuilder industry

gold.note: AP close: gold fell 3.4% to settle at $4,008.80; briefly traded below $4,000 intraday, lowest since November

pce_preview.note: Economists expect PCE prices rose 4.1% in May, highest in three years; core expected 3.4% per Investopedia/Dow Jones Newswires survey
pce_preview.context: Fed signaled considering a rate hike by year-end; CME FedWatch shows at least one hike priced by December

text: Homebuilders were among the biggest winners: KB Home surged 16.7% and D.R. Horton jumped 6.7% following approval of legislation beneficial to the industry. Gold fell 3.4% to settle at $4,008.80 an ounce, briefly trading below $4,000 for the first time since November. Investors are now focused on Thursday's PCE inflation report, with economists expecting prices to have risen 4.1% in May, which would be the highest level in three years. Fed Chair Kevin Warsh emphasized the central bank's commitment to price stability in last week's meeting.
